title: Sharpening kitchen knives to extend their working life
subtitle: Whetstone technique, honing rods, and the edge angle that determines how long an edge lasts
excerpt: A sharp knife is safer and more useful than a blunt one. Most kitchen knives go blunt from infrequent honing and incorrect sharpening rather than from wear. A whetstone and 15 minutes of practice restores most knives to working sharpness; a honing rod maintains that edge between sharpenings for months.
sourceNotes: Murray Carter and Tim Johnson knife sharpening methodology for kitchen knives. Japanese bladesmithing guild guidance on whetstone grit sequences. Kitchen Knife Forums technical consensus on European vs Japanese knife angles. Knifewear sharpening guide for home cooks. RHS guidance on sharpening garden tools by analogy for grit and angle principles.

content.text: A kitchen knife dulls in two ways. The wire edge folds over with use; honing fixes this without removing metal. The edge angle wears away over time; sharpening removes metal to reset it. Most home knives are rarely honed, so they accumulate both problems. A whetstone session followed by regular honing keeps a good-quality knife sharp for years; buying a new cheap knife every time an old one goes blunt discards a useful tool and wastes the steel and handle materials.

European knives (German and French kitchen style) are typically sharpened at 20-22 degrees per side. Japanese knives (Santoku, gyuto) at 12-17 degrees per side. A lower angle gives a keener edge but is more fragile; a higher angle gives a more durable edge for harder use. Match the angle to the knife's original bevel when restoring an edge. A simple way to find the angle: hold the knife flat on the stone (0 degrees) and raise the spine until a 1-2 mm gap appears under the blade: this is roughly 15-20 degrees for a standard kitchen knife.
Soak the stone in water for 5-10 minutes (water stones) or apply a light oil film (oil stones). A combination stone, 400 grit on one side and 1000 grit on the other, handles most sharpening tasks.
Place the stone on a stable surface (a damp cloth under it prevents slipping). Hold the knife at your chosen bevel angle.

Work the coarse side first (400 grit): stroke the edge along the stone from heel to tip in a sweeping arc, pushing the edge away from you as if trying to slice a thin layer off the stone surface. 10-15 strokes per side. Check for a wire burr on the opposite side with your thumb (drag across the spine toward the edge, not along it).
Switch sides and repeat until a burr forms on the first side again. The burr moving from side to side tells you metal is being removed evenly.
Move to the fine side (1000 grit) and repeat with 8-10 strokes per side. This removes the coarse burr and refines the edge.
Strop 10-15 strokes per side on a leather strop or the back of a leather belt. This removes the final wire burr and aligns the edge microscopically.

Hone the knife before each heavy use session: 5-6 light strokes per side on a smooth honing steel at the bevel angle. This realigns the wire edge without removing metal. A knife honed regularly after each use needs full whetstone sharpening less than twice a year under normal kitchen use.
